original_abstract:
Non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, enabling researchers to detect and annotate small molecules within highly complex environmental matrices. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) remains the most prevalent strategy for gathering product ion spectra; however, its effectiveness depends heavily on the fine-tuning of instrument parameters to balance spectral quality, metabolome coverage, and annotation rates. In this study, we systematically evaluated the impact of 10 core DDA settings on the Q Exactive HF Orbitrap platform using natural organic matter samples from marine, river, and terrestrial environments. Following data processing with MZmine and GNPS-based molecular networking (both classical and feature-based), we compared performance using metrics such as network node count, multivariate clustering, library annotation rates, and MS/MS placement accuracy.

Our findings reveal that *automatic gain control* (AGC), *microscans*, *mass resolving power*, and *dynamic exclusion* are the most critical determinants of data quality. In contrast, *collision energy*, *TopN*, and *isolation width* exerted moderate influence, while *apex trigger*, *monoisotopic selection*, and *isotopic exclusion* had only minor effects on the results. By examining the ergonomics of data acquisition on the Q Exactive platform, we provide a prioritized framework for method development. These insights offer a practical guide for researchers to optimize acquisition parameters, facilitating deeper chemical insights into environmental metabolomes and other complex biological systems.
humanized_abstract:
Data-dependent non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) is a widely used workflow in metabolomics to explore the metabolic response in organisms as well as characterizing the small molecules in the complex environmental samples. Due to its high sample throughput and broad dynamic range, data-dependent acquisition (DDA) is the default workflow, which largely depends on the optimization of MS conditions to achieve the highest possible ion intensity, as well as the widest possible range of features over the entire chromatogram and optimal number of product ions that can be used for the annotation of features. In this study, the impacts of 10 critical DDA parameters on Q Exactive HF Orbitrap UH-MS were explored using natural organic matter isolated from three different environments, i.e., seawater, river and soil, and the performance of metabolomics workflows was examined after data processing using two popular tools, i.e., MZmine and molecular networking analysis by General Network Pharmacology Specification (GNPS) in both the classical and feature-based workflows, in terms of the number of nodes, the multivariate clustering analysis and the number of library match and the accuracy of MS/MS.

Using our dataset, we identified the parameters which had the most influence on data quality, and assigned these as either primary, secondary or minimal impact variables. We found that AGC, microscans, mass resolving power and dynamic exclusion were the variables with the most impact, whilst collision energy, TopN and isolation width had a secondary impact, and that apex trigger, monoisotopic selection and isotopic exclusion had a minimal impact. This examination of the ergonomics of data acquisition on the Q Exactive mass spectrometer provides a prioritised set of parameters that experimentalists can use to construct methods that yield the best possible data for the exploration of environmental metabolomes, and other complex biological systems.
